name: Associated Types

laws: Each implementer fixes the associated type(s) for itself
Associated types are determined by the implementing type, not chosen by the caller
They participate in type inference of generic code
x.encoding: Rust trait associated types (Iterator::Item), Swift protocol associatedtype, C++ member typedefs / traits, Scala type members. Java/C# use generic type parameters instead.

def: Type members declared by an interface/trait that each implementation fills in, letting a generic contract refer to related types without extra type parameters.
problem: Expressing "this trait has an output/element type fixed by the implementer" with plain generic parameters is verbose and over-flexible.
mechanism: A trait/protocol declares an associated type (e.g. Iterator::Item); each impl specifies it. Generic code refers to Self::Item, and inference resolves it — unlike a type parameter, the implementer (not the caller) decides it.
benefits: Cleaner signatures than extra generic params
Better inference for fluent generic APIs
limits: One associated type per impl (not caller-chosen)
failures: Reaching for associated types where multiple impls per type are needed (use a parameter)
whenNot: You need many implementations differing only by the related type for one base type
myths: "Associated types and generic parameters are interchangeable" — they differ in who chooses the type
